title: Synthesis & Decomposition
subtitle: How reactants combine — and how products break apart
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: Conduct investigations to predict and identify the products of a range of reactions, including synthesis and decomposition
Predict the products of synthesis and decomposition reactions and write balanced chemical equations
lessonIntent: Students can identify a reaction as synthesis or decomposition from the equation pattern and predict and balance the products.
examSkill: Classify a reaction as synthesis (A + B → AB) or decomposition (AB → A + B) and write the balanced equation with state symbols.
productionNotes: Mirrors site lesson `subjects/chemistry/year11/module3/lesson02.html`. Real-world anchor: Beirut 2020 ammonium nitrate explosion (2,750 tonnes — same reaction controlled vs explosive). Worked examples: 2Mg + O2 → 2MgO (synthesis); CuCO3 → CuO + CO2 (decomposition). Misconception: synthesis ≠ two elements only; two compounds can combine (SO3 + H2O → H2SO4).

scenes.caption: Synthesis and Decomposition — Year 11 Chemistry, Module 3.

voiceover.text: Synthesis and Decomposition. Year eleven Chemistry, Module three.

scenes.heading: Why did Beirut explode if ammonium nitrate is just fertiliser?
scenes.body: In 2020, a Beirut warehouse exploded with the force of a small nuclear weapon. The cause: 2,750 tonnes of ammonium nitrate — a white crystalline solid sold worldwide as fertiliser. Same compound, same reaction. One pathway grows crops. The other levelled a city.
scenes.callout: What changed wasn't the chemistry — it was the conditions.
scenes.caption: Ammonium nitrate decomposes either way. Conditions decide which.

voiceover.text: Here's what makes Module 3 matter. In twenty-twenty, a Beirut warehouse exploded with the force of a small nuclear weapon. Cause: two thousand seven hundred and fifty tonnes of ammonium nitrate — a white crystalline solid sold worldwide as fertiliser. The same compound, the same reaction. One pathway feeds crops. The other levelled a city. What changed wasn't the chemistry. It was the conditions — confinement, contamination, sustained heat. Today's lesson: the two reaction patterns that explain how compounds form and how they fall apart.

scenes.heading: Synthesis vs decomposition
scenes.body: Strip away the chemistry and most reactions reduce to one of two patterns: things combining, or things falling apart. Recognise the pattern from the equation alone — count the reactants.

bullets.text: Synthesis: A + B → AB. Two or more reactants combine into one product.
Decomposition: AB → A + B. One reactant splits into two or more products.
Count reactants: 2+ reactants → synthesis. 1 reactant → decomposition.
Reactants don't have to be elements — compounds combine too.

scenes.callout: Count reactants: one means decomposition, two means synthesis.
scenes.caption: Two reactants combining = synthesis. One reactant splitting = decomposition.

voiceover.text: Strip away the chemistry and almost every reaction in this module reduces to two patterns. Synthesis: A plus B gives AB — two or more reactants combine into one product. Decomposition: AB gives A plus B — one reactant splits into two or more products. The fastest way to tell them apart is to count the reactants. One reactant on the left means decomposition. Two or more means synthesis. And the reactants don't have to be elements — compounds combine too. Sulfur trioxide plus water gives sulfuric acid is still synthesis.

scenes.heading: Recognising the pattern in equations
scenes.body: Every HSC question of this type either gives you the equation and asks for the type, or names the type and asks for the products. Learn to read both directions.

bullets.text: CuCO₃(s) → CuO(s) + CO₂(g) — one reactant → decomposition.
Always include state symbols (s, l, g, aq) — examiners check.

scenes.callout: Type from the pattern. Products from the chemistry.
scenes.caption: Two reactants → synthesis. One reactant → decomposition. Always with states.

voiceover.text: Here's how to read both directions. Two magnesium plus oxygen gives two magnesium oxide — two reactants on the left, so it's synthesis. Copper carbonate gives copper oxide plus carbon dioxide — one reactant, so it's decomposition. Sulfur trioxide plus water gives sulfuric acid — two reactants, still synthesis, even though both are compounds. And always include state symbols. Solid, liquid, gas, aqueous. Examiners check, and dropping them costs marks.

scenes.heading: Same reaction, two outcomes
scenes.body: Ammonium nitrate decomposes either gently or violently. The controlled pathway gives N₂O and water — used industrially. The explosive pathway gives N₂, O₂ and water — Beirut's pathway. Think of it like a domino chain: one push tips slowly, a hard shove tips everything at once.
scenes.callout: Confinement + sustained heat = the explosive pathway.
scenes.caption: Controlled decomposition makes laughing gas. Explosive decomposition makes a shockwave.

voiceover.text: The mechanism behind Beirut. Imagine ammonium nitrate decomposition like a domino chain — one nudge tips dominoes slowly. A hard shove tips everything at once. The controlled pathway gives nitrous oxide plus water — used in industry. The explosive pathway gives nitrogen, oxygen and water vapour — three and a half moles of gas per mole of solid. But unlike dominoes, the energy released heats more solid into reaction. Confinement plus sustained heat plus contamination flips the switch from controlled to explosive.

scenes.heading: Synthesis: balancing magnesium combustion
scenes.question: Magnesium ribbon burns brightly in air to form magnesium oxide. Identify the reaction type and write the balanced equation with state symbols.
scenes.coachNote: Target: classify the type, then balance. Two reactants combining → synthesis.
steps: Known: Mg(s) burns in O₂(g); product is MgO(s).
Find: reaction type + balanced equation with states.
Identify type: 2 reactants → 1 product → synthesis (A + B → AB).
Balance O — need 2 MgO on the right.
Now balance Mg — left has 1, right has 2. Add a 2 to Mg.
Answer = 2Mg(s) + O₂(g) → 2MgO(s). Check: 2 Mg, 2 O each side ✓.
scenes.caption: 2Mg(s) + O₂(g) → 2MgO(s). A synthesis reaction, balanced with states.

voiceover.text: Magnesium ribbon burns brightly in air to form magnesium oxide. Target: classify the reaction, then balance. Two reactants on the left — magnesium and oxygen — combining into one product. That's synthesis. Unbalanced: Mg plus O two gives MgO. Balance oxygen first by putting a two in front of MgO. Now magnesium is unbalanced — left has one, right has two. Put a two in front of Mg as well. Final balanced equation: two Mg solid plus O two gas gives two MgO solid. Two magnesiums and two oxygens on each side. Check passes.

scenes.heading: Synthesis isn't only between elements
scenes.body: Students often think: "Synthesis means two elements combining." That's the wrong instinct. The actual defining feature is one product forming from two or more reactants — and the reactants can be compounds. Instead of "two elements", read it as "two or more reactants". SO₃ + H₂O → H₂SO₄ is synthesis. CaO + CO₂ → CaCO₃ is synthesis. The textbook A + B → AB diagram is correct, just don't let the symbols mislead you.
scenes.callout: Count reactants, not the type of reactants.
scenes.caption: Compounds can combine in synthesis too. The test is one product, not element reactants.

voiceover.text: The trap. Students often think synthesis means two elements combining — A and B as bare atoms, not molecules. That's the wrong instinct. The defining feature is one product forming from two or more reactants. The reactants can be compounds. SO three plus H two O gives H two SO four — that's synthesis. CaO plus CO two gives CaCO three — also synthesis. The fix: count the reactants, don't worry about whether they're elements or compounds.

scenes.heading: Decomposition in real time
scenes.body: Heat copper(II) carbonate and it changes from green to black as it breaks down. CuCO₃(s) → CuO(s) + CO₂(g). One reactant, two products — textbook decomposition.
scenes.caption: CuCO₃(s) → CuO(s) + CO₂(g). One reactant decomposing into two products.

voiceover.text: Now decomposition in the lab. Heat green copper carbonate and watch it turn black, as CuCO three solid breaks down into CuO solid plus CO two gas. That's one reactant splitting into two products, and it's already balanced, with one copper, one carbon, and three oxygens on each side. The colour change and the gas escaping are both observable indicators that the reaction is happening.

scenes.heading: Your turn
scenes.question: Hydrogen peroxide slowly converts to water and oxygen gas: H₂O₂(aq) → H₂O(l) + O₂(g). Classify this reaction and write the balanced equation.
scenes.pausePrompt: Pause: count the reactants. Then balance.
answerSteps: Reactants on the left: 1 (H₂O₂). Products on the right: 2 (H₂O and O₂).
Atom check unbalanced: 2H, 2O left; 2H, 1+2=3O right. Oxygen unbalanced.
Balance oxygen: try 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂. Left: 4H, 4O. Right: 4H, 2+2=4O ✓.
scenes.caption: Decomposition. 2H₂O₂(aq) → 2H₂O(l) + O₂(g).

voiceover.text: Your turn. Hydrogen peroxide slowly converts to water and oxygen gas. Classify the reaction and balance the equation. Pause now and try before the answer plays. One reactant on the left, two products on the right — decomposition. Atom-counting the unbalanced equation: two H and two O left, two H and three O right. Oxygen is off. Put a two in front of H two O two on the left, and a two in front of H two O on the right. Answer: decomposition. Two H two O two gives two H two O plus O two.

scenes.heading: Summary
scenes.finalPrompt: Ask: how many reactants? One means decomposition; two or more means synthesis.
points: Synthesis: A + B → AB. Two or more reactants combine into one product.
Decomposition: AB → A + B. One reactant splits into two or more products.
Reactants in synthesis can be compounds (SO₃ + H₂O → H₂SO₄) — not just elements.
Decomposition can be controlled or explosive — same chemistry, different conditions (Beirut 2020).
Always balance with coefficients and write state symbols (s, l, g, aq).
scenes.caption: Two patterns. Five takeaways. One decision rule.

voiceover.text: Five things to remember. Synthesis: A plus B gives AB — two or more reactants combine into one product. Decomposition: AB gives A plus B — one reactant splits into two or more. The reactants in synthesis can be compounds — sulfur trioxide plus water gives sulfuric acid is still synthesis. Decomposition can be controlled or explosive — same chemistry, different conditions, as Beirut showed. And always balance with coefficients, never subscripts, with state symbols. Decision rule for the exam: count the reactants. One means decomposition, two or more means synthesis.
